goal: CFA Level I Exam - Core Content Mastery
description: Complete the curriculum and practice questions for the CFA Level I exam, focusing on high-weight areas like Ethics and Financial Statement Analysis.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Working full-time 40 hours per week; study limited to evenings and weekends.
baseline.physical_constraints: Suffer from eye fatigue; requires Blue Light filters and 20-20-20 rule breaks.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must achieve >70% in all Ethics practice modules before taking the final mock.

tasks.description: Sign up for the exam and install the CFA Institute candidate app and curriculum files.
tasks.success_criteria: Registration confirmation received and dashboard is accessible.

tasks.description: Read the Standards of Professional Conduct and complete the 200 associated practice questions.
tasks.success_criteria: Passed all Ethics quiz sets with an aggregate score of 70%.

tasks.description: Master Time Value of Money (TVM) and probability distributions using the financial calculator.
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly solved 10 TVM word problems in a row using the TI BA II Plus.

tasks.description: Review Income Statements, Balance Sheets, and Cash Flow analysis under IFRS and US GAAP.
tasks.success_criteria: Able to derive a Cash Flow Statement from a Balance Sheet without prompts.

tasks.description: Study bond valuation, duration, and equity valuation models (DDM, Multipliers).
tasks.success_criteria: Completed the End-of-Chapter questions for both sections with >65% accuracy.

tasks.description: Take the first official CFA Institute mock exam under timed, proctored-style conditions.
tasks.success_criteria: Exam completed and score report generated.

description: A daily habit of documenting technical work or learning to maintain an up-to-date public-facing portfolio (GitHub/Personal Site).

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be done after the work day ends at 5:30 PM.
baseline.non_negotiables: No sensitive employer-owned code may be uploaded.

tasks.description: Identify one code snippet, diagram, or paragraph written today that represents a lesson learned.
tasks.success_criteria: One file or concept is identified and isolated for documentation.
tasks.fallback_version: Use a generic placeholder like 'Refined CSS skills' if no specific artifact exists.

tasks.description: Remove any private API keys, company names, or internal proprietary logic from the artifact.
tasks.success_criteria: Code is verified to be safe for public release.
tasks.fallback_version: Write a text-only summary of the concept without code.

tasks.description: Add the artifact to a public 'Today-I-Learned' or 'Project-Scratchpad' repo with a clear README note.
tasks.success_criteria: GitHub contribution graph shows a green square for the day.
tasks.fallback_version: Save the file to a local 'Pending' folder and try again tomorrow.

description: Complete the 75-hour pre-license course, pass the course final, and successfully clear the state licensing examination.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Course must be completed within a 90-day window from enrollment.
baseline.physical_constraints: Requires stable internet for proctored online exams.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must achieve a minimum score of 75% on all practice quizzes before the state exam.

tasks.task: Complete Property Ownership and Land Use modules
tasks.description: Watch all video lectures and read the text for Chapters 1-4 of the pre-license curriculum.
tasks.success_criteria: Course dashboard shows modules 1-4 as 100% complete.

tasks.description: Draft a visual guide explaining Metes and Bounds, Lot and Block, and Government Survey systems.
tasks.success_criteria: A single-page diagram correctly identifies all three land description types.

tasks.task: Complete Valuation and Market Analysis modules
tasks.description: Review the Sales Comparison, Cost, and Income approaches to appraisal.
tasks.success_criteria: Completion of the module quiz with a passing score.

tasks.description: Solve 20 problems involving commission splits, proration, and loan-to-value ratios.
tasks.success_criteria: At least 16 out of 20 math problems solved correctly on the first attempt.

tasks.description: Complete the 100-question proctored course final exam to receive the certificate of completion.
tasks.success_criteria: Official digital certificate generated and saved to 'Certifications' folder.

tasks.description: Submit the course certificate to the state board and book the earliest available testing slot at a PSI center.
tasks.success_criteria: Exam appointment confirmed and added to the calendar.

description: Build stamina and accuracy for the Evidence-Based Reading section of the SAT through consistent daily passage analysis.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be done at a desk using a physical pencil for marking.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must set a timer for every passage to track speed.

tasks.description: Read one SAT-style passage (History or Science) and underline the main claim and three pieces of supporting evidence.
tasks.success_criteria: The text is physically marked with clear indicators of the thesis and evidence.
tasks.fallback_version: Read the passage and write a one-sentence summary without underlining.

tasks.description: Answer the 10-11 questions associated with the passage under timed conditions.
tasks.success_criteria: All questions are answered before the timer expires.
tasks.fallback_version: Answer only 5 questions with no timer.

tasks.description: Check answers and for every mistake, identify if it was a 'Evidence' error, 'Vocabulary in Context' error, or 'Main Idea' error.
tasks.success_criteria: Every incorrect answer has a specific error category assigned in the study log.
tasks.fallback_version: Read the correct answer explanations without logging the category.

description: Comprehensive review of nursing principles, pharmacology, and clinical judgment to obtain a Registered Nurse license.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must study around a part-time clinical rotation schedule.
baseline.physical_constraints: Requires a quiet space to practice 'Next Generation NCLEX' (NGN) case studies.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must complete the entire Kaplan or UWorld question bank.

tasks.description: Study major drug classes, side effects, and nursing considerations for cardiovascular and endocrine medications.
tasks.success_criteria: A drug reference sheet for the top 50 NCLEX drugs is created.

tasks.task: Safe and Effective Care: Management of Care
tasks.description: Review principles of delegation, prioritization, and informed consent.
tasks.success_criteria: Completed 50 practice questions on delegation with >75% accuracy.

tasks.description: Work through 5 'Next Generation' unfolding case studies focusing on clinical judgment.
tasks.success_criteria: All 5 case studies are submitted and rationale reviewed.

tasks.description: Review therapeutic communication techniques and developmental stages (Erikson/Piaget).
tasks.success_criteria: Score of >80% on the Psychosocial integrated quiz.

tasks.description: Take a Computerized Adaptive Testing (CAT) mock exam that shuts off between 75 and 145 questions.
tasks.success_criteria: Mock exam passed with a 'High' probability of passing the actual NCLEX.

description: Establish a consistent pre-study routine to reduce cognitive friction and transition into a state of deep focus.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be performed immediately before the first study session of the day.
baseline.non_negotiables: No digital devices or music allowed during the priming sequence.

tasks.description: Physically clear the desk of all items not related to the current subject.
tasks.success_criteria: The desk surface is clear and the relevant textbook/laptop is the only item present.
tasks.fallback_version: Just move one distracting item off the desk.

tasks.description: Perform 2 minutes of box breathing (4s in, 4s hold, 4s out, 4s hold) to lower heart rate.
tasks.success_criteria: Completion of at least 8 full breath cycles.
tasks.fallback_version: Take 3 deep, slow breaths.

tasks.description: Handwrite one specific goal for the upcoming study block and the primary benefit of achieving it.
tasks.success_criteria: A single sentence exists on a sticky note (e.g., 'Master 5 formulas to feel confident for Friday').
tasks.fallback_version: Mentally state the goal out loud.

tasks.description: Read the table of contents or summary of the previous session to activate relevant schemas.
tasks.success_criteria: Able to recall the last major topic studied without looking at notes.
tasks.fallback_version: Read only the titles of the last 3 pages studied.

goal: Master the 'Polya Method' for Mathematical Problem Solving
description: Internalize George Polya's 4-step problem-solving framework (Understand, Plan, Execute, Look Back) to tackle difficult non-standard math problems.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must apply the method to at least 3 problems per session.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must write out the 'Plan' step explicitly in English before writing any equations.

tasks.description: Read the summary of Polya's 'How to Solve It' and memorize the four phases: Understand the Problem, Devise a Plan, Carry out the Plan, Look Back.
tasks.success_criteria: Able to recite the 4 steps and 3 heuristic questions for each step (e.g., 'Have you seen it before?').

tasks.description: Take 5 complex word problems and ONLY write down the 'Unknown', the 'Data', and the 'Condition'. Do not solve them.
tasks.success_criteria: 5 problems have their variables and constraints clearly listed.

tasks.description: For the same 5 problems, write down the strategy name (e.g., 'Draw a Figure', 'Work Backwards', 'Solve a Simpler Problem') to be used.
tasks.success_criteria: A clear strategy is identified for all 5 problems.

tasks.description: Solve 3 'easy' difficulty competition problems using the full written framework.
tasks.success_criteria: All 3 problems solved with written evidence of all 4 Polya steps.

tasks.description: Take a previously solved problem and find a second, different method to derive the solution.
tasks.success_criteria: An alternative valid solution path is documented.

goal: Daily Chess Tactics for Pattern Recognition
description: Improve calculation speed and tactical awareness by solving specific chess puzzles daily.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use a 'real board' mode or hide the solution button.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must calculate the full variation to checkmate/material gain before moving the first piece.

tasks.description: Solve 5 simple 'Mate in 1' puzzles as fast as possible to prime the brain.
tasks.success_criteria: 5 puzzles solved correctly in under 3 minutes total.
tasks.fallback_version: Solve 2 puzzles.

tasks.description: Open one 'Hard' difficulty puzzle. Spend up to 10 minutes calculating the tree of variations without moving pieces.
tasks.success_criteria: The correct move sequence is visualized and then executed successfully.
tasks.fallback_version: Use a 'Medium' puzzle and calculate only 2 moves deep.

tasks.description: Review the 'Hard' puzzle with the computer engine to see if any defensive resources were missed.
tasks.success_criteria: One 'what if' line is explored and understood.
tasks.fallback_version: Just read the solution text.

tasks.description: Identify the tactical motif used in the puzzle (e.g., 'Pin', 'Skewer', 'Removing the Defender') and write it down.
tasks.success_criteria: The correct tactical term is logged.
tasks.fallback_version: Select the tag from a dropdown list.

goal: Resolve persistent high-latency issue in web application using Five Whys
description: Systematically identify and fix the root cause of 500ms+ database query delays during peak traffic hours.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use existing server resources; no budget for hardware upgrades.
baseline.non_negotiables: The fix must be tested in a staging environment before production deployment.

tasks.description: Query the last 24 hours of logs to isolate specific API endpoints with latency exceeding 500ms.
tasks.success_criteria: A list of the top 3 slowest endpoints is exported to a CSV file.

tasks.description: For the slowest endpoint, recursively ask 'Why?' until the underlying infrastructure or logic flaw is identified.
tasks.success_criteria: A written document exists tracing the failure from the surface symptom to a root cause (e.g., missing index).

tasks.description: Write a specific technical solution based on the root cause identified (e.g., adding a composite index or refactoring a join).
tasks.success_criteria: A code diff or database migration script is prepared.

tasks.description: Apply the fix to the staging database and run a load test to simulate peak traffic.
tasks.success_criteria: Latency on the target endpoint remains under 100ms during the 15-minute load test.

tasks.description: Merge the fix to the production branch and monitor the latency dashboard for 1 hour.
tasks.success_criteria: Production dashboard shows 95th percentile latency below 150ms.

description: Perform a daily systematic verbal walkthrough of new code to identify logic flaws and edge cases before they become bugs.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be done at the start of the final hour of the workday.
baseline.physical_constraints: Requires a quiet space where talking out loud is permitted.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must physically explain the code to an inanimate object or teammate.

tasks.description: Choose a section of code written today that contains the most complex logic or conditional branching.
tasks.success_criteria: Target code is highlighted and open in the IDE.
tasks.fallback_version: Select any single function from today's work.

tasks.description: Explain out loud exactly what each line of code is doing and what the expected inputs/outputs are.
tasks.success_criteria: Completion of the verbal walkthrough from the start to the end of the block.
tasks.fallback_version: Read the code silently while moving lips to maintain focus.

tasks.description: Identify three potential inputs that could cause the code to fail (e.g., null values, empty strings, max integers).
tasks.success_criteria: Three specific edge cases are noted in the code comments or a scratchpad.
tasks.fallback_version: Identify only one common edge case like 'null value'.

goal: Diagnose and repair a recurring basement drainage issue
description: Systematically identify the cause of water pooling in the basement during heavy rain and implement a drainage solution.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited to hand tools and a basic wet-dry vacuum; no heavy excavation machinery.
baseline.physical_constraints: Must work in a low-clearance crawl space.
baseline.non_negotiables: No modifications allowed to the shared foundation wall with the neighbor.

tasks.description: Using a garden hose, spray water on the exterior grading and foundation to pinpoint exactly where water is infiltrating.
tasks.success_criteria: The exact entry point of the water is marked with chalk on the interior basement wall.

tasks.description: Check all gutters for debris and ensure downspout extensions are directing water at least 6 feet away from the foundation.
tasks.success_criteria: Gutters are clear and water flows freely through the extensions away from the house.

tasks.description: Use a plumbing snake to clear any sediment or blockages from the main basement floor drain.
tasks.success_criteria: A 5-gallon bucket of water poured into the drain disappears immediately without backing up.

tasks.description: Clean the area around the identified crack and pack it with hydraulic cement until flush with the wall.
tasks.success_criteria: The crack is sealed and the cement is hard to the touch.

tasks.description: Add 5 bags of topsoil to the exterior area near the leak, sloping the soil away from the foundation.
tasks.success_criteria: A level placed on the soil shows a visible downward slope away from the house.

tasks.description: Re-simulate heavy rain with the garden hose for 15 minutes over the repaired area.
tasks.success_criteria: The interior basement wall remains dry to the touch throughout the test.

description: Deconstruct one complex societal or personal problem every day into its fundamental truths to develop original solutions.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be performed in a physical notebook to avoid digital distraction.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must identify at least three 'assumptions' that are usually taken for granted.

tasks.description: Identify a complex problem you usually accept at face value (e.g., 'Why is housing expensive?' or 'Why can't I wake up early?').
tasks.success_criteria: The problem is written at the top of a blank page.
tasks.fallback_version: Use a pre-written prompt from a 'Big Questions' list.

tasks.description: List all the things people 'know' to be true about this problem. Circle the ones that are not fundamental physical laws.
tasks.success_criteria: At least 3 circled assumptions are listed on the page.
tasks.fallback_version: Identify only one assumption and move on.

tasks.description: Break the problem down to its 'atoms'—the undeniable truths (e.g., 'A house is made of wood, labor, and land').
tasks.success_criteria: A list of 3-5 fundamental truths exists in the notebook.
tasks.fallback_version: List only one fundamental truth.

tasks.description: Design a new solution based *only* on the fundamental truths, ignoring the previous assumptions.
tasks.success_criteria: A short paragraph describing a non-traditional solution is written down.
tasks.fallback_version: Write down one reason why the current solution is inefficient.

goal: Master the Fundamentals of Dynamic Programming (DP)
description: Transition from brute-force recursive thinking to optimized memoization and tabulation techniques for algorithmic problems.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use Python or Java for implementations.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must verify time and space complexity for every solution derived.

tasks.description: Take the 'Fibonacci Sequence' and 'Climbing Stairs' problems and draw out the full recursive decision tree on paper.
tasks.success_criteria: A physical diagram showing redundant (overlapping) subproblems is created.

tasks.description: Solve 'House Robber' using a recursive function and a hash map/array to cache results.
tasks.success_criteria: Solution passes all test cases and avoids Time Limit Exceeded (TLE) errors.

tasks.description: Refactor the 'House Robber' solution to use an iterative loop and a DP table, removing recursion.
tasks.success_criteria: Iterative solution works correctly with O(n) time and O(n) space.

tasks.description: Further optimize the Tabulation solution to reduce space complexity from O(n) to O(1) using variables.
tasks.success_criteria: Solution uses constant space.

tasks.description: Apply the framework to 'Unique Paths' or 'Minimum Path Sum'. define the state transition equation: dp[i][j] = ...
tasks.success_criteria: Correct state transition equation written down and implemented.

tasks.description: Study the 0/1 Knapsack problem and implementing the 2D array solution.
tasks.success_criteria: Able to explain what each cell dp[i][w] represents in the matrix.

description: Develop the ability to simulate code execution mentally or on paper to improve debugging skills and logic verification.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use pen and paper; no computer execution allowed until the end.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must explicitly track the values of all variables at every iteration.

tasks.description: Find a code snippet (10-20 lines) involving nested loops, recursion, or pointer manipulation.
tasks.success_criteria: Snippet is visible on screen or printed out.
tasks.fallback_version: Use a simple 'for loop' snippet.

tasks.description: Draw a table with columns for the Line Number, Variable Names, and Output/Console.
tasks.success_criteria: Table is ready with all relevant variables listed.
tasks.fallback_version: Just list variables on the side of the page.

tasks.description: Manually 'run' the code line by line, updating the variable values in the table for a specific input set.
tasks.success_criteria: Table is filled out completely until the code terminates or crashes.
tasks.fallback_version: Trace only the first 3 iterations of the loop.

tasks.description: Run the code on a computer with the same input and compare the actual final state with the trace table.
tasks.success_criteria: The manual trace matches the computer output exactly.
tasks.fallback_version: Check against a provided solution walkthrough.

description: Create a web-based application that visualizes how Dijkstra’s and A* algorithms find the shortest path on a user-defined grid with obstacles.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use Vanilla JavaScript and HTML5 Canvas (no external game engines).
baseline.non_negotiables: The visualization must allow the user to place walls in real-time.

tasks.description: Render a 2D grid of 'nodes' on a canvas and implement a coordinate system to track start and end points.
tasks.success_criteria: A visible grid appears on screen where nodes change color when clicked.

tasks.description: Write a function to transform the grid into an Adjacency List representation for the pathfinding logic.
tasks.success_criteria: Console log shows a valid object mapping each node to its walkable neighbors.

tasks.description: Implement the core Dijkstra’s logic using a priority queue to find the shortest path from start to end.
tasks.success_criteria: The algorithm correctly identifies the shortest distance values for all reachable nodes.

tasks.description: Modify the algorithm to trigger a 'draw' function for every node visited, creating an animation of the expansion.
tasks.success_criteria: The screen shows nodes changing color sequentially as the algorithm 'searches'.

tasks.description: Implement the Manhattan Distance heuristic to upgrade the search to A* and compare it with Dijkstra’s.
tasks.success_criteria: The A* search visits significantly fewer nodes than Dijkstra’s for the same path.

description: Establish a habit of analyzing and timing different algorithmic approaches to the same problem to develop an intuition for efficiency.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use the system time library for nanosecond precision.
baseline.non_negotiables: Each session must compare at least two different time complexities (e.g., O(n²) vs O(n log n)).

tasks.description: Solve a single problem using a naive approach and an optimized approach (e.g., Bubble Sort vs Merge Sort).
tasks.success_criteria: Two working functions exist that produce the same output for identical inputs.
tasks.fallback_version: Use pre-written library functions to compare performance instead of writing from scratch.

tasks.description: Run both functions against a large input dataset (n=10,000+) and record the execution time in milliseconds.
tasks.success_criteria: A table exists showing the raw time data for both approaches.
tasks.fallback_version: Run with a smaller dataset (n=500) if time is limited.

tasks.description: Plot the results or calculate the growth rate to confirm that the empirical results match the theoretical Big O complexity.
tasks.success_criteria: A written note confirms why the optimized version was faster based on operation count.
tasks.fallback_version: Mentally verify that the faster code had fewer nested loops.

goal: Design a Scalable URL Shortener Service (System Design)
description: Create a high-level design document for a service like TinyURL that can handle 100 million new URLs per month.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must identify specific database choices (SQL vs NoSQL).
baseline.non_negotiables: Must include 'Back-of-the-Envelope' calculations for storage and bandwidth.

tasks.description: Calculate read/write QPS and total storage needed for 5 years based on 100M monthly writes and a 10:1 read/write ratio.
tasks.success_criteria: A spreadsheet exists with estimates for Traffic, Storage, Bandwidth, and Memory.

tasks.description: Define the REST API endpoints (e.g., createShortURL, getLongURL) including input parameters and HTTP return codes.
tasks.success_criteria: API specification written in standard format (Swagger/OpenAPI style).

tasks.description: Design the data model to store the mapping (ShortURL, OriginalURL, CreationDate, Expiration) and justify the choice of database.
tasks.success_criteria: Schema diagram created; decision between SQL (Relational) and NoSQL (Key-Value) justified in writing.

tasks.description: Choose a strategy for generating unique short keys (e.g., Base62 encoding of a counter vs. MD5 hash) and handle collisions.
tasks.success_criteria: Algorithm logic is written out in pseudocode.

tasks.description: Draw the full system including Load Balancers, Web Servers, Database Shards, and Caching layers.
tasks.success_criteria: A visual diagram showing the flow of a write request and a read request.

goal: Implement a Thread-Safe Bounded Blocking Queue
description: Write a custom implementation of a blocking queue (Producer-Consumer pattern) using low-level synchronization primitives to master concurrency.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use a language with explicit threading support (Java, C++, Go).
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use Condition Variables (or wait/notify) rather than high-level library queues.

tasks.description: Create the class structure with `enqueue` and `dequeue` methods and an underlying circular buffer or linked list.
tasks.success_criteria: Class compiles with the basic data structure (non-thread-safe version) working.

tasks.description: Add a Mutex (or ReentrantLock) to protect the shared state during enqueue and dequeue operations.
tasks.success_criteria: Code runs without crashing, though blocking logic is not yet implemented.

tasks.description: Add logic to `wait()` when the queue is full (producer) or empty (consumer) and `signal()` when state changes.
tasks.success_criteria: Logic correctly handles the 'queue full' and 'queue empty' edge cases.

tasks.description: Write a test script that spawns 5 producer threads and 5 consumer threads to stress-test the queue.
tasks.success_criteria: Test runs to completion without deadlocks or race conditions (data corruption).

description: Read and deconstruct one engineering blog post (e.g., Uber, Netflix, Meta) daily to learn real-world architecture patterns.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must extract the 'Problem', 'Solution', and 'Trade-off' from each article.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must draw a rough sketch of the architecture described.

tasks.description: Choose a high-quality technical article focusing on scaling, database migration, or microservices.
tasks.success_criteria: Article is open and ready to read.
tasks.fallback_version: Pick an article from 'High Scalability'.

tasks.description: Identify the specific constraint the team faced and what they sacrificed to solve it (e.g., Consistency for Availability).
tasks.success_criteria: A single sentence summary of the trade-off is written in notes.
tasks.fallback_version: Identify the main technology used.

tasks.description: Draw the system diagram on a notepad based on the text description.
tasks.success_criteria: A diagram exists showing at least 3 distinct components (e.g., Client -> Load Balancer -> Service).
tasks.fallback_version: List the components as bullet points.

description: Deep dive into the Sliding Window technique to intuitively solve substring and subarray problems without brute force.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must implement solutions in Python.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must write a reusable code template for 'Variable Size' window problems.

tasks.description: Draw the difference between a Fixed Window (e.g., max sum of size k) and Variable Window (e.g., smallest subarray with sum >= s).
tasks.success_criteria: A visual diagram exists explaining how the 'Left' and 'Right' pointers move.

tasks.description: Solve 'Maximum Average Subarray I' and 'Find All Anagrams in a String'.
tasks.success_criteria: Both problems solved with O(n) time complexity.

tasks.description: Solve 'Minimum Size Subarray Sum'. Focus on the `while` loop condition for shrinking the window.
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly implemented the expansion (right++) and contraction (left++) logic.

tasks.description: Solve 'Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters'.
tasks.success_criteria: Solution uses a Set or Map to track duplicates within the window.

tasks.description: Write a generic code skeleton that handles window expansion, constraint checking, and window shrinking.
tasks.success_criteria: A versatile template saved to the personal snippet library.

description: Simulate the pressure of a real coding interview or LeetCode Weekly Contest to build speed and stress resilience.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be done on Saturday or Sunday mornings.
baseline.non_negotiables: Zero access to Google, StackOverflow, or previous submissions during the 90 minutes.

tasks.description: Open the Virtual Contest page, put phone in 'Do Not Disturb', and start the 90-minute timer.
tasks.success_criteria: Contest interface is ready and distractions are removed.
tasks.fallback_version: Set a timer for 1 single problem (20 mins).

tasks.description: Attempt to solve the 4 contest problems (Easy, Medium, Medium, Hard) strictly within the time limit.
tasks.success_criteria: Effort applied continuously until time is up or all problems are solved.
tasks.fallback_version: Attempt only the first 2 problems.

tasks.description: Check the results to see which test cases failed and record the ranking/score.
tasks.success_criteria: Score recorded in tracking spreadsheet.

tasks.description: For one problem that was NOT solved, read the editorial and implement the solution without looking at the text.
tasks.success_criteria: One failed problem is now understood and submitted successfully.
tasks.fallback_version: Just read the approach logic.

description: Train the brain to instantly identify the correct data structure or algorithm for a problem without actually writing the code.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must cover at least 5 different problem types.
baseline.non_negotiables: Do not write code; this is purely a classification exercise.

tasks.description: Open a random LeetCode problem and read the description and constraints (e.g., n < 10^5).
tasks.success_criteria: Problem statement read and constraints noted.
tasks.fallback_version: Read the title and tags only.

tasks.description: Within 60 seconds, verbally state the pattern (e.g., 'This is Top K Elements using a Heap' or 'This is Graph BFS').
tasks.success_criteria: A specific pattern is guessed.
tasks.fallback_version: Guess the general data structure (e.g., 'Tree').

tasks.description: Reveal the 'Related Topics' tags or check the discussion titles to verify if the hypothesis was correct.
tasks.success_criteria: Hypothesis confirmed or corrected.

tasks.description: Briefly note *why* the constraints pointed to that solution (e.g., 'n=20 suggests O(2^n) recursion').
tasks.success_criteria: One reason linking constraints to complexity is noted.

description: Understand and implement backtracking to solve combinatorial problems like N-Queens, Sudoku, and Permutations.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must visualize the recursion tree for at least 3 problems.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must implement state pruning (optimizations) where applicable.

tasks.description: Draw the full state space tree for 'Permutations of [1, 2, 3]' on paper.
tasks.success_criteria: Tree diagram shows all leaf nodes and the backtracking paths.

tasks.description: Solve 'Subsets' (Power Set) using the 'include vs exclude' decision pattern.
tasks.success_criteria: Code passes all test cases with correct output.

tasks.description: Solve 'N-Queens'. Implement the `isValid` function to check diagonals and columns efficiently.
tasks.success_criteria: Function returns correct number of solutions for N=8.

tasks.description: Solve 'Sudoku Solver' but optimize by trying the cell with the fewest options first (Most Constrained Variable).
tasks.success_criteria: Solver completes a 'Hard' Sudoku puzzle under 1 second.

description: Design a system to limit API requests (e.g., 100 req/min) using Redis and sliding window logs.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must handle race conditions in a distributed environment.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must compare Fixed Window vs Sliding Window Log vs Token Bucket algorithms.

tasks.description: Create a comparison matrix of Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, and Sliding Window Counters.
tasks.success_criteria: Table exists listing Pros/Cons and memory usage for each.

tasks.description: Design the data structure (e.g., Sorted Sets) to store timestamps for the 'Sliding Window Log' approach.
tasks.success_criteria: Lua script logic or pseudo-code written for atomic operations.

tasks.description: Draw the flow: Client -> Middleware (Rate Limiter) -> Redis -> Backend Service.
tasks.success_criteria: Diagram includes handling of '429 Too Many Requests' responses.

description: Master dependency resolution problems (e.g., Course Schedule, Build Systems) using Kahn's Algorithm and DFS.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must implement both BFS (Kahn's) and DFS approaches.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must correctly detect cycles in a directed graph.

tasks.description: Define 'Indegree' and draw a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) with its indegree table.
tasks.success_criteria: Correct calculation of indegrees for a 6-node graph.

tasks.description: Solve 'Course Schedule II' using a Queue and Indegree array.
tasks.success_criteria: Code correctly returns a valid ordering or detects a cycle.

tasks.description: Implement the 'visited' and 'recursion stack' (path) arrays to detect cycles in a DFS traversal.
tasks.success_criteria: Function returns false immediately upon finding a back-edge.

description: Analyze slow SQL queries and implement specific indexing strategies (B-Tree, Hash, Composite) to reduce execution time.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` (Postgres) or equivalent to verify results.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must demonstrate the difference between a Full Table Scan and an Index Scan.

tasks.description: Populate a table with 1 million rows of random user data.
tasks.success_criteria: Database table is populated.

tasks.description: Run a `SELECT * FROM users WHERE last_name = 'Smith'` query and record the execution time and plan.
tasks.success_criteria: Query plan confirms a Full Table Scan.

tasks.description: Create a B-Tree index on `last_name` and re-run the query.
tasks.success_criteria: Query time reduced by at least 90% and plan shows Index Scan.

tasks.description: Test a query filtering by `last_name` AND `age`. Compare separate indexes vs a single composite index.
tasks.success_criteria: Composite index validated as more efficient for the multi-column query.

goal: Implement a Trie (Prefix Tree) for Auto-Complete
description: Build a Trie data structure from scratch to handle string insertion and prefix searching efficiently.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must define a custom `TrieNode` class.
baseline.non_negotiables: Search complexity must be O(L) where L is the word length.

tasks.description: Define the `TrieNode` with a HashMap (or array size 26) of children and a boolean `isEndOfWord`.
tasks.success_criteria: Class compiles.

tasks.description: Implement the method to add a word by iterating through chars and creating nodes if they don't exist.
tasks.success_criteria: Able to insert 'apple' and 'app' correctly.

tasks.description: Implement `startsWith(prefix)` to return true if any word in the Trie begins with the given string.
tasks.success_criteria: Returns True for 'app' after inserting 'apple'.

description: Learn one bitwise operation trick per day to optimize code (e.g., checking parity, swapping without temp, isolating bits).

baseline.fixed_constraints: No calculator allowed; verify binary math on paper.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must apply the trick to a coding problem.

tasks.description: Study one specific operator (e.g., XOR `^`, Left Shift `<<`) and its common use case (e.g., `x & (x-1)` clears the lowest set bit).
tasks.success_criteria: Concept understood and written in notes.

tasks.description: Solve one 'Bit Manipulation' tagged problem on LeetCode (e.g., 'Number of 1 Bits', 'Single Number').
tasks.success_criteria: Problem solved optimally using bitwise ops.

description: Master the usage of Min-Heaps and Max-Heaps for 'Top K' problems and median maintenance.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must implement a custom Heap class (push/pop/heapify) at least once.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must distinguish when to use a Heap vs Sorting.

tasks.description: Write a Min-Heap using an array implementation, handling the `bubbleUp` and `sinkDown` logic.
tasks.success_criteria: Heap property acts correctly after 10 random insertions.

tasks.description: Solve 'Top K Frequent Elements' using a Min-Heap of size K to keep track of the largest elements seen so far.
tasks.success_criteria: Solution complexity is O(N log K).

tasks.description: Solve 'Find Median from Data Stream' using two heaps (one Min, one Max).
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly balances elements between the two heaps.

description: Implement two fundamental Creational Design Patterns to manage object instantiation.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be thread-safe implementations.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must explain *why* these patterns are used (e.g., decoupling, resource control).

tasks.description: Write a Thread-Safe Singleton (e.g., using 'Double-Checked Locking' in Java or `sync.Once` in Go).
tasks.success_criteria: Multiple threads retrieve the exact same instance ID.

tasks.description: Create a `ShapeFactory` that returns objects of type `Circle`, `Square`, or `Rectangle` based on string input.
tasks.success_criteria: Client code creates shapes without using the `new` keyword directly.

goal: Master Binary Search on Answer Space
description: Learn to apply Binary Search not just to arrays, but to solution ranges (e.g., 'Minimize the Maximum' problems).

baseline.fixed_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must define the monotonic condition (predicate function) clearly.

tasks.description: Recognize the pattern: If checking `x` is valid implies `x+1` is valid (or vice versa), binary search applies.
tasks.success_criteria: Written explanation of monotonicity.

tasks.description: For 'Koko Eating Bananas', write the helper function `canEatAll(speed)` that returns true/false.
tasks.success_criteria: Helper function works correctly in isolation.

tasks.description: Implement the binary search loop (left, right, mid) calling the predicate function.
tasks.success_criteria: Problem solved optimally.

description: Revisit old solutions or personal projects to improve readability, modularity, and variable naming.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be done on Sunday evenings.
baseline.non_negotiables: Code must not break existing functionality (regression testing).

tasks.description: Open a project or LeetCode submission from >1 month ago.
tasks.success_criteria: Target code is open.

tasks.description: Identify 'Magic Numbers', massive functions, or unclear variable names (e.g., `temp`, `x`, `arr`).
tasks.success_criteria: List of 3 things to improve.

tasks.description: Extract helper functions, rename variables to be descriptive, and add comments where logic is complex.
tasks.success_criteria: Code is cleaner and passes tests.

goal: Master Monotonic Stack and Queue Patterns
description: Learn to solve 'Next Greater Element' and 'Sliding Window Maximum' problems using monotonic data structures to achieve O(n) performance.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use Python or C++ for implementation.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be able to explain the logic for why elements are popped (maintaining the 'monotonic' property).

tasks.description: Trace the stack state for the array [2, 1, 2, 4, 3] to find the 'Next Greater Element'.
tasks.success_criteria: A step-by-step table showing the stack contents after each element is processed.

tasks.description: Solve 'Next Greater Element I' on LeetCode using a decreasing stack and a hash map.
tasks.success_criteria: Solution passes all test cases in O(n) time.

tasks.description: Use a Deque to maintain a monotonic decreasing window to solve 'Sliding Window Maximum'.
tasks.success_criteria: Code correctly identifies the maximum in every window shift without O(n*k) complexity.

description: Master the two-pointer technique for detecting cycles and finding midpoints in linear data structures.

baseline.fixed_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must derive the mathematical proof for why the pointers meet in a cycle.

tasks.description: Solve 'Linked List Cycle'. Implement the logic where `fast` moves twice as fast as `slow`.
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly identifies presence of cycle without extra space (O(1) space).

tasks.description: Solve 'Linked List Cycle II'. Prove and implement the logic to find the start of the loop.
tasks.success_criteria: Code returns the correct node and the math behind 'distance p = distance k' is understood.

description: Learn to manage disjoint sets and detect connectivity in graphs with nearly constant time complexity.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must implement Path Compression and Union by Rank/Size.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be able to explain why the time complexity is the Inverse Ackermann function.

tasks.description: Write a `UnionFind` class with `find` and `union` methods.
tasks.success_criteria: The class successfully merges sets of integers.

tasks.description: Solve 'Number of Connected Components in an Undirected Graph'.
tasks.success_criteria: Solution utilizes the Union-Find class to count isolated clusters.

tasks.description: Solve 'Redundant Connection' by finding the edge that creates a cycle in a tree.
tasks.success_criteria: Correct edge is returned by identifying when `find(u) == find(v)`.

description: Learn to identify when a locally optimal choice leads to a globally optimal solution, focusing on Interval Scheduling and Jump Game problems.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must prove the greedy choice property logic (even informally) before coding.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must sort intervals correctly based on end times or start times depending on the problem.

tasks.description: Solve 'Non-overlapping Intervals'. Sort by *end time* to maximize the number of non-overlapping intervals.
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly implemented sorting and the single-pass iteration.

tasks.description: Solve 'Merge Intervals'. Sort by *start time* and merge overlapping ranges linearly.
tasks.success_criteria: Code handles the edge case where one interval completely swallows another.

tasks.description: Solve 'Jump Game' using a greedy `max_reachable` index. Avoid Dynamic Programming (O(n^2)) in favor of O(n) greedy.
tasks.success_criteria: Solution passes in O(n) time.

description: Move beyond simple recursion to handle Tree Serialization and O(1) Space Traversals (Morris Traversal).

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must implement iterative solutions using a Stack.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must handle null values correctly during serialization.

tasks.description: Implement a codec to convert a tree to a string (e.g., '1,2,X,X,3,X,X') and back using Preorder traversal.
tasks.success_criteria: The deserialized tree matches the original structure exactly.

tasks.description: Implement Inorder traversal without recursion or a stack by creating temporary links to predecessors (Threading).
tasks.success_criteria: Code traverses the tree using O(1) auxiliary space.

tasks.description: Solve 'Vertical Order Traversal of a Binary Tree' using BFS and a sorting strategy for column/row indices.
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly handles nodes at the same position by sorting by value.

description: Build a Segment Tree to handle Range Sum Queries with updates in logarithmic time, overcoming the limitations of Prefix Sum arrays.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must implement array-based tree representation (index `2*i` and `2*i+1`).
baseline.non_negotiables: Must achieve O(log n) for both update and query operations.

tasks.description: Write the `buildTree` function to recursively divide the array range and store sums in the tree array.
tasks.success_criteria: Tree array is populated correctly with the sum of the full range at index 1.

tasks.description: Implement the `update(index, val)` function to modify a leaf node and recalculate sums up to the root.
tasks.success_criteria: Update reflects correctly in the root sum.

tasks.description: Implement `query(left, right)` to return the sum of a specific range by combining relevant tree nodes.
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly returns sums for arbitrary ranges [L, R].

description: Implement a Least Recently Used (LRU) Cache from scratch using a Doubly Linked List and a Hash Map to achieve O(1) performance for both get and put operations.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must not use built-in OrderedDict or LinkedHashMap libraries.
baseline.non_negotiables: The cache must maintain a fixed capacity and evict the least recently used item when full.

tasks.description: Define the `Node` class for a doubly linked list (key, value, prev, next) and initialize the `LRUCache` class with dummy head and tail nodes.
tasks.success_criteria: A linked list structure exists where head.next = tail and tail.prev = head.

tasks.description: Implement `add_node` (to head) and `remove_node` logic to handle the pointer updates for the doubly linked list.
tasks.success_criteria: Pointers are updated correctly without losing references or causing memory leaks.

tasks.description: Implement the `get` method to move accessed items to the head and the `put` method to insert new items or update existing ones, handling eviction if capacity is reached.
tasks.success_criteria: The cache correctly tracks usage and evicts the item nearest to the tail.

goal: Network Flow: Max Flow Min Cut
description: Master the Ford-Fulkerson method and Edmonds-Karp algorithm to solve maximum flow problems in flow networks.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must implement the algorithm using an adjacency matrix or list to represent capacities.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be able to identify the minimum cut from the saturated edges after finding the max flow.

tasks.description: Draw a small 4-node network and manually construct its residual graph after a single augmenting path is found.
tasks.success_criteria: Correct representation of forward (remaining) and backward (reverse) capacities.

tasks.description: Implement the Ford-Fulkerson method using BFS to find the shortest augmenting path (Edmonds-Karp).
tasks.success_criteria: The algorithm correctly calculates the total max flow for a sample network.

tasks.description: Run a final BFS/DFS on the residual graph to find all nodes reachable from the source; the edges leaving this set constitute the Min-Cut.
tasks.success_criteria: The capacity of the identified cut equals the value of the maximum flow.

description: Identify and fix logical bugs in provided 'broken' code snippets to sharpen analytical skills and knowledge of language-specific pitfalls.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be done without using an IDE debugger (manual tracing only).
baseline.non_negotiables: Must provide a fix that passes original test cases.

tasks.description: Read a snippet containing common errors (e.g., off-by-one, integer overflow, improper scoping) and find the flaw.
tasks.success_criteria: The line number and type of logical error are identified.
tasks.fallback_version: Read a 'Bug of the Day' explanation from a blog.

tasks.description: Rewrite the function or modify the specific lines to resolve the issue.
tasks.success_criteria: The corrected code successfully processes a dry-run input.

description: Learn to solve optimization problems involving small sets (n < 20) by using bitmasks to represent states in Dynamic Programming.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must implement in a language that supports bitwise operators efficiently (Python/C++).
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be able to calculate the total number of states ($$2^n \times n$$) before implementation.

tasks.description: Practice checking if the i-th bit is set, setting the i-th bit, and toggling bits using `&`, `|`, and `^`.
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly implemented helper functions for bit manipulation.

tasks.description: Solve the TSP using DP with Bitmask to find the shortest possible route that visits every city exactly once.
tasks.success_criteria: Code correctly identifies the minimum path cost for a graph with 15 nodes.

tasks.description: Solve a problem where N tasks are assigned to N people with varying costs, minimizing the total cost.
tasks.success_criteria: Successfully used a 1D DP array where the index is the bitmask representation of assigned tasks.

goal: Balanced Search Trees: Red-Black Tree Rotations
description: Master the self-balancing logic of Red-Black trees, focusing on left/right rotations and color flipping.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must maintain the 5 core Red-Black properties during all operations.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must manually trace a sequence of 10 insertions on paper before coding.

tasks.description: Draw a 3-node unbalanced tree and show the step-by-step pointer updates for a Left Rotation.
tasks.success_criteria: The final drawing shows the parent-child pointers correctly reassigned.

tasks.description: Implement the `insertFixUp` method that handles the 3 cases: Uncle is Red, Uncle is Black (Line), and Uncle is Black (Triangle).
tasks.success_criteria: A tree of 20 random integers remains balanced and satisfies all Red-Black properties.

description: A daily habit of taking a working $O(n^2)$ solution and refactoring it to $O(n \log n)$ or $O(n)$ using advanced data structures.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use a profiler or simple timer to prove the performance gain.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must write a one-sentence explanation of the 'bottleneck' in the original version.

tasks.description: Analyze a slow solution and identify the redundant operation (e.g., nested loop searching for a value).
tasks.success_criteria: The line(s) causing the $O(n^2)$ complexity are identified.

tasks.description: Replace the slow part with a Hash Map, Heap, or Binary Search to reduce the time complexity.
tasks.success_criteria: The new solution passes all test cases and shows a visible reduction in execution time for large inputs.
tasks.fallback_version: Read a 'Top Solution' on LeetCode and explain its optimization.

goal: Master B-Tree Data Structures for Database Engine Design
description: Understand the multi-way search tree logic used in production database indexing (like MySQL and PostgreSQL) to manage disk-resident data.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must focus on the 'Degree' (m) of the tree and its effect on height.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must manually trace the 'Node Splitting' and 'Borrowing/Merging' logic for deletions.

tasks.description: Draw a B-Tree of degree 3 and explain why every node (except root) must have at least $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ children.
tasks.success_criteria: A diagram exists showing keys and pointers correctly balanced according to B-tree rules.

tasks.description: Trace the insertion of 10 keys that force at least two node splits. Document the 'median promotion' step.
tasks.success_criteria: The final tree is perfectly balanced and maintains the search property.

goal: Implement a Suffix Trie for Fast Pattern Matching
description: Build a data structure that allows for $O(m)$ substring searches where $m$ is the pattern length, regardless of the text size.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must handle the termination character (e.g., '$') correctly.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be able to find all occurrences of a pattern in a large text.

tasks.description: Given a string like 'BANANA$', list all suffixes and insert them into a standard Trie.
tasks.success_criteria: All suffixes (e.g., 'A$', 'NA$', 'ANA$') are correctly identified.

tasks.description: Implement a search function that traverses the Trie based on a query string.
tasks.success_criteria: Function correctly identifies if 'NAN' exists within 'BANANA'.

description: A daily 15-minute habit of sketching a complex data structure (e.g., AVL Tree, Skip List) from memory to solidify architectural intuition.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use physical paper and pencil; no undo button.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must label all pointers (next, prev, parent, child) and state variables (height, color, balance factor).

tasks.description: Use a randomizer to pick one data structure from a curated list of 20.
tasks.success_criteria: A specific structure is selected.

tasks.description: Draw the structure containing at least 7 nodes, ensuring all internal properties are respected.
tasks.success_criteria: The drawing is complete with all necessary labels and pointers.
tasks.fallback_version: Copy the structure from a reference for 5 minutes.

tasks.description: Compare the sketch to a textbook reference and circle any errors (e.g., a missing null pointer or incorrect balance factor).
tasks.success_criteria: The sketch is graded as 'Perfect' or 'Requires Review'.

goal: Implement a Skip List Data Structure
description: Build a probabilistic alternative to balanced trees that supports search, insertion, and deletion in O(log n) time.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must implement in Java or C++ to handle manual pointer references.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must include a visual print method to verify the layered structure.

tasks.description: Define the `SkipNode` class containing the value and an array (or list) of 'forward' pointers for different levels.
tasks.success_criteria: Class compiles with a constructor accepting a value and level.

tasks.description: Implement the coin-flip logic to determine the height of a new node (e.g., 50% chance to promote to the next level).
tasks.success_criteria: Function returns a level between 1 and MAX_LEVEL following a geometric distribution.

tasks.description: Write the `search(value)` method that starts at the top level and moves down/right to find the target.
tasks.success_criteria: Search correctly returns true/false for existing/non-existing values in a static test list.

tasks.description: Implement `insert(value)`: Find the update path, create the new node, and re-link pointers at every level up to the node's height.
tasks.success_criteria: Node is reachable via search and appears in the correct sorted position.

tasks.description: Implement `delete(value)`: Locate the node and update predecessor pointers to skip over it at all levels.
tasks.success_criteria: Node is removed and the list structure remains intact (no broken links).

tasks.description: Create a `printList()` method that displays the list level-by-level to verify the tower structure.
tasks.success_criteria: Output clearly shows the hierarchy (e.g., Level 3: 10 -> 50 -> NULL).

goal: Master Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree) Data Structure
description: Learn to implement and apply Binary Indexed Trees (BIT) to solve range query problems with O(log n) updates, replacing slower Segment Trees for specific use cases.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must rely on bitwise operations (LSB extraction) rather than explicit tree pointers.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must verify the 1-based indexing requirement common in BIT implementations.

tasks.description: Write a short comparison between Prefix Sum Arrays (O(1) query, O(n) update) and Fenwick Trees (O(log n) for both).
tasks.success_criteria: A written table exists comparing time complexities for Read vs Update operations.

tasks.description: Practice the isolation logic `x & (-x)` by manually calculating the 'parent' index for 5 random integers on paper.
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly identified the index updates for numbers like 12 (1100 -> 1000) and 7 (0111 -> 0110).

tasks.description: Implement `update(index, delta)` to add a value to an element and propagate the change up to relevant 'ancestors' using the LSB addition logic.
tasks.success_criteria: Function correctly modifies the internal array without index out of bounds errors.

tasks.description: Implement `query(index)` to traverse down from the given index to 0 by subtracting the LSB to accumulate the sum.
tasks.success_criteria: Function returns the correct sum for indices [1...k] on a test array.

tasks.description: Implement `range_query(left, right)` using the formula `query(right) - query(left - 1)`.
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly calculates sums for arbitrary sub-segments.

tasks.description: Solve the 'Count Inversions' problem (finding how many pairs (i, j) exist where i < j and A[i] > A[j]) using a BIT to track frequencies.
tasks.success_criteria: Algorithm runs in O(n log n) compared to the O(n^2) brute force baseline.

goal: Establish a Daily Algorithmic Pattern Recognition Habit
description: Develop a consistent routine for identifying and implementing fundamental data structure patterns to maintain technical interview readiness.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Study sessions must occur between 7:00 AM and 8:30 AM to avoid workday fatigue.
baseline.physical_constraints: Must use a laptop with a blue light filter to prevent eye strain.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must write at least one line of code per session; reading only is not permitted.

tasks.description: Select one specific algorithmic pattern (e.g., Sliding Window, Two Pointers, or BFS) from a curated list.
tasks.success_criteria: The pattern is chosen and a corresponding problem is opened in the browser.
tasks.fallback_version: Use the 'Problem of the Day' on a coding platform regardless of pattern.

tasks.description: Identify three key constraints in the problem (e.g., time complexity requirements or input size) that justify the chosen pattern.
tasks.success_criteria: A brief written note exists linking the constraints to the pattern choice.
tasks.fallback_version: Identify only one constraint (e.g., 'Array size is 10^5, so I need O(n)').

tasks.description: Draft the logical flow of the solution using comments, focusing on edge cases like empty inputs or single-element sets.
tasks.success_criteria: The structure of the loop or recursion is outlined in the editor.
tasks.fallback_version: Draw a small example on paper and trace the logic manually.

tasks.description: Translate the pseudo-code into working code and run it against the provided test cases.
tasks.success_criteria: The code passes all sample test cases provided by the platform.
tasks.fallback_version: Read a high-quality solution and manually type it out line-by-line while narrating the logic.

tasks.description: Review the runtime and memory usage; identify one specific way to reduce space or time overhead.
tasks.success_criteria: A finalized version of the code is saved with at least one optimization comment included.
tasks.fallback_version: Read the top-rated discussion post to see a more efficient approach.

goal: Build a Deep Learning Library from Scratch
description: Implement a minimal autograd engine and neural network library in Python using only NumPy to understand backpropagation and tensor operations.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Cannot use PyTorch, TensorFlow, or JAX.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must successfully train a multi-layer perceptron to solve the MNIST digit classification problem.

tasks.description: Implement a Value class that stores a scalar and its gradient, supporting basic arithmetic (+, *) and building a computational graph for backpropagation.
tasks.success_criteria: The engine correctly calculates the derivative of a complex nested expression compared to manual calculus.

tasks.description: Generalize the scalar engine to handle multi-dimensional NumPy arrays (Tensors) and implement vectorized addition and matrix multiplication.
tasks.success_criteria: A forward pass of a linear layer (W * x + b) completes without shape mismatch errors.

tasks.description: Implement ReLU, Sigmoid, and Softmax activation functions along with Cross-Entropy loss including their local gradients.
tasks.success_criteria: The loss function outputs a positive scalar and gradients can be backpropagated through activations.

tasks.description: Build a 'Module' base class, a 'Linear' layer, and an SGD optimizer that updates weights using the stored gradients.
tasks.success_criteria: Model weights change after a single call to optimizer.step().

tasks.description: Create a data loader for MNIST, initialize a 2-layer network, and run a training loop for 5 epochs.
tasks.success_criteria: Training accuracy reaches at least 90% on the test set.

description: Maintain a consistent high-accuracy habit for GRE quantitative reasoning focusing on geometry, algebra, and data interpretation.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use the official GRE on-screen calculator emulator.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must not spend more than 1 minute and 45 seconds on any single question.

tasks.description: Solve 10 random Quant problems from the Manhattan Prep or Official ETS pool.
tasks.success_criteria: All 10 problems answered within 18 minutes.
tasks.fallback_version: Solve 5 problems with no time limit.

tasks.description: Identify 3 problems where 'D' (cannot be determined) is the correct answer and explain the logic.
tasks.success_criteria: A written note exists detailing why specific variable values (like 0 or negative numbers) lead to 'D'.
tasks.fallback_version: Read the explanation for one Quant Comparison error made today.

tasks.description: Document any incorrect answers in a master spreadsheet, categorizing by concept (e.g., 'Prime Numbers' or 'Triangles').
tasks.success_criteria: Spreadsheet is updated and at least one 'Lesson Learned' is written.
tasks.fallback_version: Mentally identify why the error occurred.

tasks.description: Review 20 flashcards for common fraction-to-decimal conversions and squares up to 25.
tasks.success_criteria: 100% accuracy on the flashcard deck.
tasks.fallback_version: Review 5 cards.

description: Develop a simple disk-based storage engine that supports basic SQL operations, indexing, and persistent storage.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use C or Rust for memory safety and manual pointer management.
baseline.non_negotiables: Data must persist on disk after the program exits.

tasks.description: Implement a Pager that reads/writes fixed-size pages (4KB) from a binary file to memory.
tasks.success_criteria: Successfully reading a specific byte from a file via the Pager.

tasks.description: Define the binary layout of a leaf node, including headers (type, parent, key count) and cells (key, value).
tasks.success_criteria: Code exists to serialize/deserialize a node to a raw byte buffer.

tasks.description: Implement a schema definition and a compact binary format to store user rows (ID, Username, Email).
tasks.success_criteria: A row can be written to a buffer and read back with 100% data integrity.

tasks.description: Create a cursor that can iterate through all rows in the B-Tree leaf nodes from start to finish.
tasks.success_criteria: Program prints all 10 inserted test rows in order.

tasks.description: Implement a simple Command Line Interface that parses 'insert' and 'select' statements using string splitting.
tasks.success_criteria: The engine processes a manual insert command and stores it on disk.

goal: Establish a Daily Morning 'Code Kata' Habit
description: Solve one small, logic-focused coding problem every morning to improve syntax fluency and edge-case thinking.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be done between 7:30 AM and 8:00 AM.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must not use AI assistants or search for the solution until the task is complete.

tasks.description: Choose a 6 kyu or 7 kyu level problem from CodeWars or a 'Simple' tagged problem on LeetCode.
tasks.success_criteria: Problem description is read and understood.
tasks.fallback_version: Redo a previously solved 8 kyu problem.

tasks.description: Write 3 test cases, including one 'happy path' and two edge cases (e.g., empty string, negative number).
tasks.success_criteria: Three manual test cases are written in the code editor comments.
tasks.fallback_version: Use only the provided default test cases.

tasks.description: Write the function to pass all test cases.
tasks.success_criteria: Code runs and returns the expected output for all test cases.
tasks.fallback_version: Read the solution logic and write it out line-by-line.

goal: Prepare for Professional Cloud Architect Certification
description: Pass the Google Cloud Professional Cloud Architect exam by mastering resource management, networking, and high availability design.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Exam date is set for day 31.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must pass two full-length practice exams with >80% before sitting for the real exam.

tasks.description: Review differences between GCE, GKE, App Engine, and Cloud Functions. Study Cloud Spanner vs Cloud SQL use cases.
tasks.success_criteria: A decision matrix table exists comparing latency, scalability, and cost for each storage type.

tasks.description: Study VPC Peering, Shared VPCs, Cloud Interconnect, and Hybrid Connectivity options.
tasks.success_criteria: Correctly explained the difference between a Global Load Balancer and a Regional one in the study notes.

tasks.description: Read the three official GCP exam case studies (e.g., Mountkirk Games) and map solutions to their business requirements.
tasks.success_criteria: A written technical solution proposal exists for each case study.

tasks.description: Take a 50-question timed practice exam in a silent room.
tasks.success_criteria: Exam completed and results analyzed for weak domains.

tasks.description: Review every missed question from Practice Exam A and watch deep-dive videos on those specific topics.
tasks.success_criteria: A new set of flashcards exists for every missed concept.
